section.title: Woe to the False Teachers
section.sectionRationale: Jude's indictment reaches full heat. A prophetic woe links the intruders to Cain, Balaam, and Korah, then six vivid word pictures expose their emptiness and danger. Enoch's ancient prophecy seals their judgment, and a final portrait names them for what they are: grumblers, boasters, and flatterers.
section.thesis: The false teachers are ripe for judgment: they walk the paths of the great rebels, they are as empty and dangerous as reefs, waterless clouds, and wandering stars, and the Lord is coming with His holy ones to convict all their ungodliness.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the woe and the three rebels, verse 11; six word pictures of their emptiness, verses 12 to 13; and Enoch's prophecy of the Lord's coming judgment, with a closing portrait, verses 14 to 16.

units.label: The woe and the three rebels

verses.text: {{ouai|Woe}} to them! They have traveled the path of Cain; they have rushed headlong into the error of Balaam; they have perished in Korah's {{antilogia|rebellion}}.

These men are {{spilas|hidden reefs}} in your love feasts, shamelessly feasting with you but shepherding only themselves. They are clouds without water, carried along by the wind; fruitless trees in autumn, twice dead after being uprooted.
They are wild waves of the sea, foaming up their own shame; wandering stars, for whom {{zophos|blackest darkness}} has been reserved forever.
Enoch, the seventh from Adam, also {{propheteuo|prophesied}} about them: “Behold, the Lord is coming with myriads of His holy ones
to execute judgment on everyone, and to convict all the {{asebes|ungodly}} of every ungodly act of wickedness and every harsh word spoken against Him by ungodly sinners.”
These men are discontented {{gongystes|grumblers}}, following after their own lusts; their mouths spew arrogance; they flatter others for their own advantage.

ouai.gloss: Woe, an exclamation of grief and impending doom. It is the cry the prophets and Jesus Himself raised over the unrepentant. Jude pronounces it over the intruders, marking them as under God's certain judgment.

antilogia.gloss: Contradiction, dispute, rebellious opposition. Korah's antilogia was his defiance of God's appointed leaders, Numbers 16. The false teachers share his spirit, setting themselves against God given authority and perishing in the same rebellion.

spilas.gloss: A hidden reef or rock just beneath the surface. Such reefs wreck ships that never see them coming. The intruders share the church's love feasts while secretly endangering the whole fellowship, a hidden hazard among trusting believers.

zophos.gloss: Gloom, deep darkness, the blackness of the nether world. Like wandering stars that give no reliable light, the false teachers are destined for the blackest darkness reserved forever, a fitting end for those who lead others astray.

propheteuo.gloss: To prophesy, to speak forth a message from God about what is to come. Jude says Enoch prophesied about these very men, applying an ancient word of coming judgment directly to the intruders of his own day.

asebes.gloss: Ungodly, irreverent, without fear of God. The word hammers four times in verse 15, ungodly people, ungodly acts, ungodly way, ungodly words. It is the defining mark of those Enoch foresaw and Jude condemns.

gongystes.gloss: A grumbler, a discontented murmurer. It echoes Israel's grumbling in the wilderness. Beneath the intruders' arrogant, flattering speech lies a restless discontent with God, chasing their own desires rather than trusting His will.

basic.context: Jude pours out a series of condemnations. He cries woe over the false teachers and links them to three infamous rebels: Cain the murderer, Balaam who taught Israel to sin for money, and Korah who defied God's leaders. Then he paints six pictures of their emptiness and danger. Finally he quotes a prophecy of Enoch that the Lord is coming to judge all the ungodly, and closes with a portrait of grumbling, boasting flatterers.
basic.hermeneutics: This is prophetic, poetic denunciation, meant to strip away any glamour from the intruders. The most discussed point is verse 14, where Jude quotes a line from the book of 1 Enoch, a Jewish writing outside Scripture. Jude affirms that particular saying as a true word about judgment, without treating the whole book as inspired, much as Paul quotes a pagan poet to make a true point.
basic.originalLanguages: Jude cries ouai, woe. Korah's sin was antilogia, rebellious defiance. The teachers are spilas, hidden reefs, doomed to zophos, blackest darkness. Enoch propheteuo, prophesied, of the Lord's coming to convict all the asebes, the ungodly, a word repeated four times in verse 15. At heart they are gongystes, grumblers, discontented with God.
basic.typology: Cain, Balaam, and Korah are types of the false teacher: envy and hatred, greed and seduction, and proud rebellion against authority. Their ends are warnings. And Enoch's prophecy points beyond the intruders to the final coming of the Lord with His holy ones to judge the whole earth.
basic.christ: The Lord who comes with myriads of His holy ones to execute judgment is, in the New Testament's light, Jesus Christ returning in glory. The same Jesus who came in humility to save will come in majesty to judge every ungodly word and deed. The false teachers who deny Him will face Him.
basic.god: God is the righteous judge who keeps a perfect record. Verse 15 stresses that He will convict every ungodly act and even every harsh word spoken against Him. Nothing is overlooked. His judgment is neither hasty nor forgetful; it is certain, thorough, and just.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 4 tells of Cain, Numbers 22 to 24 and 31 of Balaam, and Numbers 16 of Korah. Second Peter 2 again parallels these condemnations. And the picture of the Lord coming with His holy ones to judge echoes Zechariah 14 and Matthew 25.
basic.oneStory: The rebels of the old covenant, Cain, Balaam, Korah, live on in every generation of those who corrupt God's people. Jude sets them under the ancient promise, carried even by Enoch, that the Lord will come to judge all ungodliness, a promise that finds its focus in the return of Christ to convict the wicked and vindicate His own.

context.literary: A crescendo of condemnation. Verse 11 packs three historical rebels into one woe. Verses 12 to 13 give six escalating images from land, sky, and sea: reefs, waterless clouds, fruitless trees, wild waves, wandering stars. Verses 14 to 15 climax with a prophetic oracle, and verse 16 lands with a plain character sketch. It is Hebrew style denunciation in Greek dress.
context.historical: Cain, Balaam, and Korah were stock examples of wickedness in Jewish teaching. The love feasts, verse 12, were shared fellowship meals of the early church, where the intruders fed themselves shamelessly. Verse 14 quotes 1 Enoch 1:9, a well known Jewish apocalyptic work that Jude's readers would recognize.
context.canonical: The three rebels and the six images root Jude deeply in the Old Testament, while the Enoch citation shows Jude engaging the Jewish literature of his day. The oracle of the Lord's coming to judge ties the passage to the whole Bible's expectation of a final reckoning fulfilled in Christ's return.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose the false teachers as empty, dangerous, and doomed by likening them to notorious rebels and to natural images of barrenness and peril, and to declare their certain judgment through Enoch's prophecy of the Lord's coming.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes and denounces the intruders; it does not prescribe imitation. Its abiding force is a warning: do not walk the path of Cain, Balaam, or Korah, and reverence the Lord who comes to judge every ungodly deed and word.

debates.question: How can Jude quote the book of 1 Enoch in verses 14 to 15?

views.label: Affirming a true prophecy without canonizing the book
views.case: Jude cites a specific saying, 1 Enoch 1:9, as a genuine word of coming judgment. Inspiration can affirm a true statement wherever it is found, just as Paul quotes the pagan poets Aratus and Epimenides in Acts 17:28 and Titus 1:12 to assert something true. Endorsing one true line is not endorsing the whole work, and the early church never received 1 Enoch as Scripture, apart from the Ethiopian tradition.

views.label: Treating Enoch as historically inspired prophecy
views.case: A minority hold that Enoch, the seventh from Adam, truly uttered this prophecy, later preserved in the book, so Jude quotes an authentic ancient word rather than the book as such. This still stops short of canonizing 1 Enoch.

debates.bearing: On either view, Jude's inspired use of the quotation does not make 1 Enoch Scripture. The Spirit affirms the truth of this saying about the Lord's coming judgment, and the orthodox church has consistently kept 1 Enoch outside the canon while receiving Jude within it. What Jude vouches for is the truth quoted, not the book quoted.

debates.question: What do the three rebels, Cain, Balaam, and Korah, represent?

views.label: Three facets of the false teachers' sin
views.case: Cain pictures hatred and a false approach to God, Balaam pictures greed that leads others into sin, and Korah pictures rebellion against God appointed authority. Together they diagnose the intruders' hearts.

views.label: A single escalating way of apostasy
views.case: The three form a path, way, error, rebellion, a downward road from envy to greed to open defiance, ending in destruction like Korah's.

debates.bearing: Both readings expose the intruders as heirs of the Bible's archetypal rebels, and both press the warning: their end, like Korah's, is destruction.

study.originalLanguages: The passage bristles with charged words. Jude opens with ouai, woe, the prophetic cry of doom. Korah's sin is antilogia, defiant contradiction of God's order. The teachers are spilas, hidden reefs that wreck the unwary, and their end is zophos, the blackest darkness, reserved forever. Enoch propheteuo, prophesied, of the Lord coming to convict the asebes, and the ungodly word drums four times in verse 15, branding every part of their lives. Verse 16 unmasks them as gongystes, grumblers, whose proud, flattering mouths cannot hide a heart discontented with God, chasing its own lusts.

typology.type: Cain, the way of hatred and false worship
typology.fulfillment: A type of those who approach God on their own terms and hate the righteous, fulfilled in the false teachers who oppose the true gospel, 1 John 3:12.

typology.type: Balaam, greed that leads others into sin
typology.fulfillment: A type of teachers who exploit God's people for gain and entice them to immorality, fulfilled in the intruders' love of money and license, Revelation 2:14.

typology.type: Korah, rebellion against God given authority
typology.fulfillment: A type of those who defy the Lord's appointed order and perish, a warning realized in the destruction awaiting the false teachers, Numbers 16.

study.christ: Enoch's prophecy declares, Behold, the Lord is coming with myriads of His holy ones to execute judgment. Read in the full light of the New Testament, this is the return of the Lord Jesus in glory with His angels to judge the living and the dead, Matthew 25:31. The Christ who first came in lowliness to bear judgment for sinners will come again to convict all who persist in ungodliness. Those who now deny Him as Master will one day meet Him as Judge, while His own are vindicated at His appearing.
study.god: God is revealed as the perfectly righteous Judge. Verse 15 emphasizes the completeness of His justice: He will convict everyone, of every ungodly deed, and even of every harsh word spoken against Him. Nothing escapes His notice and nothing is left unaddressed. This is fearful for the ungodly and comforting for the faithful, for it means evil will finally be named, judged, and undone by a God who forgets nothing and overlooks no wrong.

crossReferences.note: Korah's rebellion against Moses and Aaron, and the destruction that swallowed the rebels, echoed in Korah's rebellion.
Balaam, whose greed and counsel led Israel into sin, the pattern of teachers who corrupt for gain.
Parallel condemnation naming Balaam and describing waterless springs and mists driven by a storm.
The Son of Man comes in glory with all His angels to judge the nations, fulfilling the oracle of the Lord coming with His holy ones.

study.oneStory: The great rebels of Israel's story, Cain, Balaam, and Korah, are not relics; their spirit lives in every corrupter of God's people. Jude places the intruders under the ancient and unbroken promise that the Lord will come with His holy ones to judge all ungodliness. That promise runs from Enoch through the prophets to its fulfillment in the return of Christ, who convicts the wicked, silences every ungodly word, and gathers His kept and faithful people to Himself.

sermon.title: Lessons from the Past
sermon.why: From Begg's series A Study in Jude, this message treats Jude 1:11-13, the woe over the false teachers, the way of Cain, Balaam, and Korah, and the six vivid word pictures of their emptiness. A clear, orthodox handling of this movement's warnings.

quiz.q: Why does Jude link the false teachers to Cain, Balaam, and Korah?

options.text: They are heirs of the Bible's great rebels, and their end will be destruction like Korah's.
options.note: Jude 1:11 diagnoses their sin through three archetypal rebels and their doom.

options.text: To show the teachers were direct descendants of these men.
options.note: The link is one of spirit and pattern, not physical descent.

options.text: Because these three were the only sinners in the Old Testament.
options.note: They are chosen as vivid examples, not because they were the only sinners.

options.text: To celebrate the courage of Cain, Balaam, and Korah.
options.note: The opposite: Jude condemns their way, he does not celebrate it.

quiz.q: What do the six word pictures in verses 12 to 13 reveal about the false teachers?

options.text: They are empty, dangerous, and doomed, like reefs, waterless clouds, and wandering stars.
options.note: The images stack up their barrenness, peril, and reserved judgment.

options.text: They are fruitful and reliable guides.
options.note: The images say the opposite: fruitless, waterless, and wandering.

options.text: They are harmless if simply ignored.
options.note: Hidden reefs are precisely the danger you do not see coming; they are far from harmless.

options.text: They are merely a little disappointing.
options.note: The images convey serious danger and certain doom, not mild disappointment.

quiz.q: How should we understand Jude quoting the book of 1 Enoch?

options.text: He affirms a true saying about judgment without making the whole book Scripture.
options.note: Inspiration can affirm a true statement wherever found, as Paul quotes pagan poets.

options.text: He proves that 1 Enoch belongs in the Bible.
options.note: The church has consistently kept 1 Enoch outside the canon while receiving Jude.

options.text: He made an error by quoting a non biblical book.
options.note: Jude's use is deliberate and inspired, affirming the truth of the quoted line.

options.text: He cites an ancient prophecy his readers would recognize.
options.note: True, but the fuller point is that he vouches for the truth quoted, not the book.

quiz.q: What does Enoch's prophecy in verses 14 to 15 announce?

options.text: The Lord is coming with His holy ones to judge and convict all the ungodly.
options.note: Jude 1:14-15 declares certain, thorough judgment on every ungodly deed and word.

options.text: That God will overlook the false teachers' sins.
options.note: The prophecy stresses conviction of every ungodly act, not overlooking sin.

options.text: That judgment is only for deeds, never for words.
options.note: It explicitly includes every harsh word spoken against God.

options.text: That the Lord's coming is uncertain and far off.
options.note: The tone is one of certainty; the Lord is coming to judge.

quiz.q: What does verse 16 reveal lies beneath the teachers' proud and flattering speech?

options.text: A discontented, grumbling heart that chases its own desires rather than trusting God.
options.note: Jude 1:16 exposes grumblers who follow their own lusts behind the arrogant words.

options.text: A genuine, humble love for the church.
options.note: Their flattery serves their own advantage, not the good of the church.

options.text: A harmless eccentricity of personality.
options.note: It is moral and spiritual rebellion, not mere personality.

options.text: A sincere but misguided zeal for God.
options.note: The text names self serving lust and discontent, not zeal for God.

questions: Cain, Balaam, and Korah fell through hatred, greed, and rebellion against authority. Which of these roots do you most need to guard your own heart against?
Hidden reefs endanger those who never see them coming. How can you and your church grow more discerning about influences that look safe but are not?
Enoch's prophecy says the Lord will convict every ungodly word. How does knowing that even our words are seen by God shape the way you speak this week?
Beneath the teachers' boasting lay a grumbling discontent with God. Where does discontent quietly drive your own choices, and how might trust in God replace it?
The Lord is coming with His holy ones to judge and to vindicate His people. How does the certainty of His return steady you when evil seems to flourish now?

translationNotes.note: Korah's rebellion renders a phrase, the antilogia of Korah, meaning his defiant opposition to God's appointed leaders in Numbers 16.
Hidden reefs translates spilas; a footnote notes the alternative rendering blemishes. Love feasts were the shared fellowship meals of the early church.
This quotes 1 Enoch 1:9, a Jewish work outside the canon; Jude affirms the truth of the saying about the Lord's coming judgment without endorsing the whole book.
The word ungodly appears four times, ungodly people, ungodly deeds, ungodly way, and ungodly words, underscoring the completeness of the coming judgment.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Jude 1:11-16, including the poetic lines of verses 14-15. No dashes appear in this movement's text.
